title: A large but transient carbon sink from urbanization and rural depopulation in China
methods: random-forest carbon density mapping (MODIS MCD43A4 + static biomass benchmark)
settlement-distance gradient analysis (Euclidean, using Global Urban Footprint)
data_sources: Tencent location service mobile phone data (2017)
Global Carbon Project fossil CO2 emissions (2000-2019)

data_scope.spatial_coverage: China national (excl. Xinjiang, Qinghai, Tibet and areas <5 people/km2)
data_scope.spatial_resolution: primary analysis at 1 km; land cover at 30-300 m; GUF at ~12 m

core_conclusion: China's mass rural-to-urban migration (-14 million rural residents/yr) generated a large aboveground carbon sink of 0.28 ± 0.05 PgC/yr during 2002-2019 by relieving human pressure on forests near depopulating settlements. Urban greening offset the initial carbon loss from urban expansion, yielding a net urban carbon balance of +0.03 PgC. However, tree-cover growth is saturating and the sink is transient — only deep cuts in fossil-fuel CO2 emissions can achieve China's 2060 carbon-neutrality goal.
why_it_matters: Reframes urbanization as a climate lever; quantifies the rural-depopulation ecological dividend at national scale; disentangles three co-occurring drivers (urban expansion, outmigration, afforestation); issues a timely saturation warning for the carbon sink; shows food-security impact was marginal (only 3.8% cropland decline).
why_nature: Directly addresses China's 2060 carbon-neutrality pledge and UN SDGs; integrates five independent satellite streams with census and mobile phone data across China at 1 km; counterintuitive but empirically robust finding challenging established consensus; bridges urban studies, forest ecology, and climate mitigation.
added_value_to_my_research: MGWR/SHAP attribution of biomass change to outmigration vs climate vs afforestation at 1 km pixel level
Rural building vacancy + biomass recovery overlay -> physical correlate of depopulation for agent-based modeling
Carbon-aware green-space accessibility metric linking healthy cities and ecosystem carbon
Migration origin-destination network -> community detection to identify next afforestation hotspots
Coupled land-use/carbon/transport scenario -> bridges compact development, job-housing networks, and EV charging equity

related.title: A strong but uneven increase in urban tree cover in China over the recent decade
related.relation: Directly extends the urban greening signal using high-resolution satellite tree detection; complementary spatial explainability of which cities gained the most biomass

related.title: Satellite mapping of every building's function in urban China reveals deep built environment disparities
related.relation: Provides the physical building-vacancy layer to ground-truth rural settlement shrinkage detected via population decline

related.title: The role of informal ruralization within China's rapid urbanization
related.relation: Documents informal urban agriculture (chengshi kaihuang) as a counterpart to rural outmigration; connects intra-urban land informality to biomass dynamics

related.title: Large disagreements in estimates of urban land across scales and their implications
related.relation: Addresses uncertainty in urban land extent estimates from GlobeLand30 and ESA-CCI — directly relevant to this paper's urban area delineation methodology

related.title: Co-benefits of transport demand reductions from compact urban development in Chinese cities
related.relation: Provides the fossil-emission side of the ledger missing from this paper's carbon accounting; quantifies how urban form affects CO2 from transport in Chinese cities

figures.caption: Population change and urbanization in China — demographic trends, ESA-CCI urban extent, and calibrated DMSP/OLS nighttime lights 1990-2020
Land use/cover systems and MODIS carbon density change — urban-agricultural-forest gradient, tree cover and biomass change by class, urban carbon stocks, and agricultural pressure maps
Population migration and aboveground carbon density change by distance to settlements — peak biomass recovery at 2-4 km, validated with Tencent spring-festival mobile phone data
Carbon density, tree cover, and NTL trends by population density change group — contrast between depopulating and rapidly urbanizing areas before and after 2000
Land cover transitions and fossil carbon emissions — Sankey of artificial surface expansion vs new forests, and the race between the carbon sink and CO2 emission trajectories
